data.title: HCA Healthcare's Health Check-Up Reveals Opportunity As Earnings Near
data.description:
In the time that I have been writing about HCA Healthcare, Inc. (HCA), things have gone quite well for the company. The last article that I wrote about the business was published in April of last year. I acknowledged that there were certain regulatory uncertainties that could negatively affect the business. But even so, I maintained that the stock was attractively priced and that there was a real opportunity for continued growth. Since reaffirming it as a ‘buy’ candidate in that article, the stock has risen 53.4%. That's well above the 36.8% increase that the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. But that's nothing. Since I originally rated it a ‘buy’ back in August of 2021, the stock has jumped 104.1%. That dwarfs the 61.7% increase that the S&P 500 experienced over the same window of time.

Fundamentally speaking, the business is doing a fine job. Revenue, profits, and cash flows are all rising. The number of licensed beds that it has increased. Occupancy rates have risen. And the revenue that it is getting from each equivalent admission continues to grow as the number of visits to its locations rises. When you add all of this together and combine it with the fact that shares still look cheap on an absolute basis, though they are perhaps closer to being fairly valued compared to other similar firms, I think that maintaining it as a very soft ‘buy’ makes sense here.

This does not mean that my opinion can't or won't change as new data justifies it. It just so happens that, before the market opens on April 24th, management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. As we approach that date, analysts believe that revenue and earnings will both increase. But of course, investors should always be diligent. The picture can always change, sometimes rapidly. So it would be wise to pay attention to what news does come out. Unless something serious happens, however, or unless the stock price rises nicely in response to its earnings release, I suspect that keeping it as a soft ‘buy’ will make sense.

HCA Healthcare Is Looking Healthy

In the event that you have never looked at HCA Healthcare before, it is helpful to know exactly what the business is and what it does. Management describes the company as one of the leading healthcare services firms in the US. It operates 190 hospitals, with 169 of those being general acute care hospitals. Seven are behavioral hospitals. And four are rehabilitation hospitals. The firm also operates 121 freestanding ambulatory surgery centers and 31 freestanding endoscopy centers. All of these assets are spread across 19 states, plus England of all places. With a footprint this large, it's no wonder that the company has such a significant market capitalization. As of this writing, it's worth $109.1 billion. That is absolutely massive.

Over the last three years now, management has achieved some attractive growth. From 2023 through 2025, revenue jumped from $65 billion to $75.6 billion. This rise in revenue is the result of multiple factors. First and foremost, over the last three years, the number of licensed beds that the company has grown from 49,588 to 50,436. On top of this, the firm saw a small increase in occupancy from 72.3% to 73.1%. There have been some other fascinating indicators as well. Take, if you will, the number of emergency room visits that the business has exhibited. Back in 2023, these came in at 9.34 million. But by 2025, the number had risen to 9.95 million. Inpatient surgery cases grew during this time also, climbing from 528,845 to 545,405. This is not to say that every category saw growth. The number of outpatient surgery cases, for instance, actually contracted from 1.04 million to 1.02 million. But on the whole, this did not stop equivalent admissions from expanding from 3.79 million to 4.11 million.

I do think it's worth also pointing out that, in addition to seeing a rise in the number of admissions, the company has also benefited from an increase in revenue per equivalent admission. Driven in large part by a rise in inpatient revenue per admission, the company saw an increase in revenue per equivalent admission from $17,149 to $18,407. This certainly played a material role in boosting overall sales. And as revenue for the company increased, profitability rose also. Net profits, as an example, jumped from $5.24 billion to $6.78 billion. Adjusted net profits expanded from $5.25 billion to $6.76 billion. We saw operating cash flow grow from $9.43 billion to $12.64 billion. If we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a jump from $9.99 billion to $12.22 billion. And finally, EBITDA for the business expanded from $12.73 billion to $15.57 billion.

As the company has expanded, management has made some interesting choices. The firm has, for years now, paid out dividends. But those have always been quite small. Over the last three years, it has paid out only $2.03 billion to shareholders this way. I know that to some investors that might seem disappointing. But not to me. I actually prefer a focus on growth and share buyback opportunities. And according to the data provided, the company has achieved not only growth but has also accomplished significant share buybacks. In fact, it went from buying back $3.81 billion worth of shares in 2023 to buying back $10.07 billion in 2025. Over the last three years, the total value of share buybacks amounted to $19.92 billion. This totaled 59 million shares of stock.

Now normally, when I see this kind of buyback activity, I do become concerned about the prospect that the business in question might be taking on too much leverage. But that doesn't appear to be the case here. If anything, the growth in EBITDA has been strong enough to cause overall leverage to drop even as net debt increased. From 2023 through 2025, net debt expanded from $38.66 billion to $45.45 billion. But the net leverage ratio for the company ticked down slightly from 3.04 to 2.92. So if anything, the company is getting healthier, even if only marginally so.

When it comes to the 2026 fiscal year, management expects even more good times. They anticipate that revenue will grow to between $76.5 billion and $80 billion. Even the low end of this range would be up from the $75.6 billion the company reported last year. This is not all, though. Net profits should come in at between $6.495 billion and $7.035 billion. At the midpoint, this would mean profitability that is virtually flat. However, EBITDA is forecasted to rise from $15.57 billion to $16 billion year over year. This would likely translate to adjusted operating cash flow of around $12.56 billion.

If we use these estimates, we can see in the chart above how the stock is priced on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar companies. These are actually pretty attractive multiples if you ask me. Though relative to other similar firms, the stock is closer to being in the fair value range. Three of the five companies I compared it to ended up being cheaper than it is on a price-to-earnings basis. Two of them were cheaper on a price-to-operating cash flow basis. Three of them, meanwhile, ended up being cheaper on an EV-to-EBITDA basis, while another was basically tied with it.

Even though the relative valuation of the company indicates that I should be more neutral than bullish, I cannot help but to remain optimistic considering all of the facts that I covered already. Meaningful share buybacks at a low multiple, continued growth, and a reduction in leverage look beautiful. But that doesn't mean that my opinion cannot change as new data justifies it. As I mentioned at the outset of this article, management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year before the market opens on April 24th. The current expectation is that revenue will come in at $19.07 billion. That would be up nicely from the $18.32 billion that the company reported a year earlier. Earnings per share, meanwhile, should grow from $6.45 to $7.23. That would mean a jump in net income from $1.61 billion to $1.73 billion. Meanwhile, adjusted earnings per share should grow from $6.45 to $7.15. That would translate to a rise in adjusted net income from $1.61 billion to $1.71 billion. The table below also shows certain cash flow metrics for the first quarter of last year. If revenue and profitability both increase, these cash flow figures almost certainly will also.

There are a couple of other things that investors should keep an eye out for when new data does come out. The first is that my estimates assume that the number of shares outstanding for the company will remain unchanged compared to what they were at the end of last year. However, the company did, in their final quarter earnings, announce a new $10 billion share buyback program, plus they raised the dividend from $0.72 to $0.78 each quarter. So we could see from this a further reduction in the number of shares outstanding. The other thing that investors should be paying attention to would be certain operational changes that the company has exhibited.

Even though management acknowledged certain uncertainties, such as the expired and enhanced premium tax credits, as well as certain issues regarding Medicaid supplemental payment programs and the Rural Health Transformation program, they stressed that their core business remains strong and that they should see a 2% to 3% annual growth in volume moving forward.

They also said that they continue to benefit from investments in technology, digital transformation, and operational improvements, all aimed at not only boosting revenue but also achieving specific margin targets. But the resiliency initiatives that the company is working on will face challenges from some of the aforementioned issues that I mentioned already. One good example that I did not mention would be the pause of the Texas Medicaid supplemental payment program that already contributed to a $250 million to $450 million expected decline in net benefits to the business. It's not unthinkable that, as market conditions continue to evolve, the firm could face some challenges here.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that's available right now, it is my belief that HCA Healthcare remains a relatively attractive prospect. The easy money has certainly been made, but management is doing a fine job even as uncertainty grows. I do think that investors would be wise to keep a close eye as new results roll in. But as the picture stands right now, I am comfortable enough to maintain a soft ‘buy’ rating on the stock.

data.title: Elanco Animal Health: Relying On Innovation To Drive Growth
data.description:
Shares of Elanco Animal Health Incorporated (ELAN) have been settling around the $25 mark after the company really saw its performance come through last year, driven by a focus on internal innovation.

In November I concluded what a difference and innovation a year made, as organic growth returned, convincingly driven by a line-up of innovative products, making those leverage concerns move to the background. That innovative growth is continuing and accelerating, yet it results in relatively modest earnings growth, which makes me a bit cautious here.

Comforting is that sales and margins are seen accelerating in the years to come, even seen accelerating, but that requires real execution, making me a bit reserved here on the valuation at current levels.

Other interesting IPOs are discussed more extensively at Value In Corporate Events.

A Very Strong Year

In February, Elanco reported a 6% increase in full-year sales to $4.71 billion, with organic growth rates coming in a point higher. Fourth quarter report sales rose as much as 12%, with organic growth accelerating to 9%. I like the composition of organic growth, driven by volumes, which rose by 8% amidst just a percent increase in pricing. Some $892 million of these revenues were classified as so-called innovation revenues.

While the topline sales trends look quite encouraging, adjusted earnings of $473 million were up just $21 million on the year before, with adjusted earnings growth trailing topline sales momentum. This made it so that adjusted earnings rose by three cents to $0.94 per share.

Adjusted EBITDA was reported at $901 million for the year, as leverage ratios were reported at 3.6 times. Adjusted EBITDA was actually down $9 million, driven by the divestment of the aqua business, as comparables from hereon get easier.

The company guided for total sales to advance to $4.95-$5.02 billion in 2026, with innovation sales seen up to $1.15 billion. This suggests just over a quarter of a billion increase in sales in this category, which reveals that the remainder of the portfolio sees flattish sales. Sales growth and the lapsing of the aqua divestment should make it so that EBITDA will increase to $970 million, plus or minus $15 million. This should allow adjusted earnings to increase to $1.03 per share, with net leverage seen down to 3.2 times by year-end.

Some Perspective

With shares trading around the $23 mark, shares of Elanco trade around 23 times adjusted earnings going forward, all while organic growth is solid, yet still some debt is employed.

By now shares are back to the levels last seen in the spring of 2022. Shares came into free fall at the time, following a 2020 deal to merge with Bayer's animal health business. That mega deal followed the 2018 separation of Elanco, when the company was spun off from Eli Lilly (LLY). This company was inspired by a successful spin-off of Zoetis (ZTS) by Pfizer (PFE) a couple of years before.

The Bayer deal was set to add $1.7 billion in sales, valued at around 4.5 times sales, resulting in demanding valuations, as the integration and organic growth have been lackluster ever since. The debt overhang and soft performance made the shares fall to the $10 mark in 2023 and 2025, followed by a recovery ever since.

An encouraging news event was a $1.3 billion sale of the aqua business last year, but more helpful was an increase in the momentum of the innovation brands, which showed more impressive revenue growth.

Getting Better

The reality is that Elanco is doing a lot better, as the question remains how much of a recovery has now been priced in. Trading at a low-20 times earnings multiple, valuations represent a premium, also given the debt employed, as investors like the mid- to high-single-digit organic growth trajectory.

This is driven by innovative brands, including a rapid increase in sales of Credelio Quattro, which reached a $100 million annual sales number just eight months after its launch, as reported in the autumn of last year. More upbeat news on the pipeline was announced, including Zenrelia and others. Growth is driven by the "Big 6," which comprise the aforementioned drugs, AdTab, Befrena, Bovaer, and Experior. The innovation revenues are seen comfortably above a billion in 2026, seen doubling by 2028.

Despite the momentum, EBITDA margins and, therefore, profit margins are barely seen outpacing reported sales growth. This makes me a bit reserved, while the 2028 guidance is rather convincing. With innovation revenues seen doubling through that year, absent of any growth in the remaining portfolio, revenues should come in around $6 billion at such point. Some 200-350 basis points of EBITDA improvements are projected, as margins might improve to 21.0%-22.5% of sales in such a case.

At the midpoint, that might result in a $1.3 billion EBITDA number. Absent of real growth in business and thus depreciating expenses, the company could see about $300 million in pre-tax earnings improvements. Such improvements could easily push up earnings to $1.50 per share at that point, creating a real roadmap for even accelerating earnings growth here.

This is encouraging, and based on this number, shares trade at 16 times forward earnings, about two years in time. That could easily result in further upward pressure on the share price, but it requires real work and accelerating earnings growth in 2027 and 2028 compared to the current trends.

Notably, this expected and even acceleration-guided force makes me feel a bit uneasy. Given all this, I am glad to see the company having turned the corner, making me a happy holder of a tiny long position but unwilling to add here.

data.title: Truist Still A 'Show Me Story,' Now With A More Challenging Macro Situation
data.description:
Apart from some progress toward a more benign regulatory environment, operating conditions for large regional banks like Truist Financial Corporation (TFC) aren’t substantially better than when I last wrote about the stock. If anything, ongoing concerns about inflation and the U.S. economy have made for a more challenging macro situation, and competition in Truist’s key Southeastern market continues to ramp up.

Truist shares are up about 2% since my last update, lagging the broader bank indices, as well as specific rivals like PNC (PNC) and U.S. Bancorp (USB). While management has put forth relatively bullish longer-term targets, including 16%-plus return on tangible common equity in 2028 and beyond, recent results have relied heavily on taxes and trading, with some weakness in core results.

I remain skeptical about management’s plans to reverse years of share loss in its core banking markets, but I do see attractive opportunities in areas like investment banking and trading, wealth management, and treasury management, and Truist has ample surplus capital to support the business. Valuation is not so demanding, but with a more modest outlook for loan and spread leverage in 2026, I don’t think investors need to rush to own the name.

A Better Quarter, But Still With Nits To Pick

Truist’s first quarter results were better than the fourth quarter in terms of outperforming Street expectations and the quality of the earnings, but it was not the sort of strong, clean quarter that would support a “things really are different now” sort of call.

The first quarter saw revenue rise about 5% year over year and contract about 2% quarter over quarter, a slight beat relative to the Street (around $0.015/share of the bottom-line beat). Net interest income rose less than 3% yoy and contracted 3% qoq, missing by about a penny, with both lower earning assets (up 2% yoy and <1% qoq) and net interest margin (up 1bp yoy and down 5bp qoq to 3.02%) relative to sell-side expectations.

Fee-based revenue was considerably stronger, growing almost 12% yoy and up slightly sequentially, beating by about $0.025/share. The investment banking and trading operations drove all of the upside (the other line-items were basically in-line or slightly better than expected), growing 36% yoy and 11% qoq. With trading becoming an increasingly important part of the business mix, Truist management really needs to start disclosing more detail about the breakdown between i-banking and trading.

Operating expenses rose about 3% yoy and contracted 2% qoq, coming in about 1% better than expected and adding about $0.02 to earnings, though with an in-line efficiency ratio (57.4%).

Pre-provision profits rose almost 9% yoy and contracted 2% qoq, beating by more than $0.03/share, more than reversing the $0.05-cent miss in the fourth quarter. Provision expense was slightly higher than expected, while taxes were once again substantially lower (adding more than $0.06/share to the beat).

Tangible book value per share rose about 7% yoy and contracted 1% qoq, while CET1 was sequentially stable at 10.8%; Truist remains one of the most-capitalized banks of its peer group, which can support substantial capital returns to shareholders and/or reinvestment in the business (like expanding trading capabilities).

Weaker Results Within The Numbers Are A Modest Concern

Looking at the underlying drivers of the business, I’m a little concerned about the direction of Truist’s business today.

Loans were up less than 1% sequentially in a quarter where large banks saw closer to 2% sequential growth on average. That’s not a large gap, but part of the bull thesis here rests on the idea that leadership and strategy changes in the Wholesale Banking business will drive share gains and above-average loan growth.

Building on that, C&I loan growth of 1.6% was softer than its peers. While management did talk about prioritizing quality over volume and I can believe that some companies have held back on expansion plans in the face of increased global and economic uncertainties, the stronger performance from other in-market rivals like PNC is a concern to me.

The deposit side of the balance sheet is also showing more evidence of competition, with total deposits up less than 1% QOQ and non-interest-bearing deposits down about 2%. Overall deposit costs are still good on a relative basis (at 1.55%), and Truist’s historical concentration on commercial lending has generated stronger asset yields and spreads, but this is a “watch item” for the remainder of the year as it does look like competition for deposits is heating up in multiple Southeastern markets.

On a more positive note, the bank’s credit quality still looks healthy. The bank has moved more aggressively than some to resolve CRE lending issues, and I don’t expect non-depository financial institution lending to be a problem. This area has come under increased scrutiny in recent months as private loans have started going bad, and there are ongoing worries that sectors like software will see even higher default rates. With less than 1% of NDFI loans going to business development companies, and below-average exposure overall, this is more of a concern for banks like PNC, First Citizens (FCNCA), and Wells Fargo (WFC) than Truist.

The Outlook

With a relatively rare liability-sensitive balance sheet, Truist is skewed to rate cuts driving a steeper yield curve. Unfortunately, recent developments, like the armed conflict with Iran, have renewed concerns about inflation and dimmed prospects for rate cuts. With that, management has lowered NII growth guidance (from 3%-4% to 2%-3%) on reduced rate cut expectations.

As far as the other businesses go, a weaker economic outlook and more caution from corporations is likely to put a damper on M&A and IPO activity, though the volatility could be good for Truist’s growing trading operations.

Looking ahead, I still have my doubts about Truist’s ability to reverse years of share loss in its key banking markets. The restructuring of the Wholesale Banking business was needed, and I like the ongoing tech/digital investments into the consumer business, but I’m concerned that management is not addressing the real issues.

Specifically, I believe a host of banks have out-competed Truist on the basis of stronger customer service. Truist has some of the lowest consumer satisfaction scores in its Southeastern markets among large banks, and I think management needs to address this more openly and directly. I like the moves to roll out additional treasury management and payment products and to focus on improved cross-selling in areas like wealth management, but if customers fundamentally don’t enjoy doing business with you, you’re not going to get very far. To that end, I’d like to hear more from management acknowledging these past deficits and discussing how they’ll fix it.

My model for Truist hasn’t changed all that much. I’m looking for around 5% to 6% core earnings growth over the next five years and long-term growth around 4%. The underlying modeling assumptions are conservative relative to management’s 16%+ ROTCE targets, but I need to see more progress, particularly in core banking operations, to move in line with those goals.

Discounted earnings gets me to a fair value around $53. ROTCE-driven P/TBV and a P/E-based approach average out to around $54, with a 12-month ROTCE of 14.5% supporting a $56/share fair value and an 11.5x multiple on my ’26 estimate getting me to just under $52. There are arguments for a higher multiple more in line with sector averages (closer to 12-12.5x), but I think Truist’s near-term rate sensitivity and loan/deposit competition can argue for a modest discount.

The Bottom Line

I like what Truist is doing in areas like investment banking and trading, and I do see good growth prospects in capital markets, wealth management, and treasury services (including payments). I’m still less confident in the plans to regain share and reignite growth in core banking, but Truist does at least have the advantages of brand recognition, an extensive branch network, and substantial capital to reinvest in improving the business.

I could argue for a fair value as high as $56 for the next 6-12 months, and that’s not bad for the sector. Still, I am concerned that economic growth could disappoint, weighing on loan demand, while inflation could limit rate cuts. Coupled with ongoing concerns about management focus, it’s enough to keep more “neutral” than “buy” for the time being.

data.title: W. P. Carey: A New Growth Strategy Comes With Trade-Offs
data.description:
Introduction

Back in August 2025, I wrote my last article on W. P. Carey (WPC) where I provided reasons to either buy, hold or sell. Buyers - among others - would be drawn to the stock because of the high single-digit expected return and inflation protection due to CPI-linked lease structures. There were good reasons to give the stock a 'hold' rating as well due to the fact that shares were trading around fair value (more on the way I measure this in this article). I also wrote the case for selling this stock. It had everything to do with so-called 'opportunity costs': the return you "lost" by not going after more lucrative market opportunities. Back in August, I did not see compelling reasons to sell the stock myself.

Including dividends, WPC returned around 25% in the last twelve months. This is a very good return when I compare it to many of my other REIT holdings.

I sold my position last year because of the sharp share price appreciation, combined with the earlier mentioned opportunity costs. It made me swap positions. WPC still remains a core position in the family portfolios that I manage.

Today, I will show you why I still believe WPC to be a good REIT for many investors. And for those wondering: Yes, it is still a REIT that I have on my watchlist.

Stock valuation

When you take a look at Mr. Market's historic valuation of WPC, you can see whether a stock is somewhat over- or undervalued. It certainly is not some sort of 'hard science', but it gives us some idea what Mr. Market currently thinks of a REIT. Most triple net REITs like WPC can be analyzed best by looking at the adjusted funds from operations (AFFO). Compared to funds from operations (FFO), AFFO excludes the non-cash straight-line rent from FFO (shown in the quarter-on-quarter financial data just below) to provide us with a better picture of the dividend safety. It is worthwhile to check the stock-based compensation, which is a non-cash item that can have a meaningful impact on the per-share AFFO growth. Regarding WPC, this number is not that meaningful.

In the graph below, you can see the normalized P/AFFO multiple of WPC between 2008 - today. The average P/AFFO multiple right now sits at 13.88x. The black line is the price of the stock, and I've highlighted the P/AFFO multiple in red, just to show you how volatility in its valuation differs over time.

When you take a look at the Q4 2025 supplemental presentation (page 7), the Q4 2025 AFFO per share in Q4 2025 was $1.27, or $5.08 when annualized. At the current price of $74, the current AFFO multiple sits at 14.56x.

My point? WPC seems to be trading just a bit above its average P/AFFO multiple, but not by a wide margin. This means that the stock is trading around its fair value, especially when considering that many REITs - today - are trading below their historic average.

When you look at the AFFO above, you could argue that growth is a bit wobbly. When I covered the Q4 2024 results last year (and maintained my buy rating for good reasons), I shared management's statement regarding the full year 2025 AFFO guidance. Management expected this number to arrive at $4.82 - $4.92. On the back of the anticipated full year investment volume of $1 billion - $1.5 billion.

The 2025 AFFO came in at $4.97, above the upper range. This is meaningful, because I prefer a management team that underpromises and overdelivers.

When you take a look at the 2026 guidance, management expects AFFO to come in between $5.13 - $5.23, on the back of an anticipated investment $1.25 - $1.75 billion investment volume. A 6.3% year-over-year growth at the midpoint. All of this made it possible for WPC to raise the dividend by 4.5% year-over-year.

When you take the 2026 AFFO guidance of $5.20 (around the upper range of the 2026 guidance) and apply the current AFFO multiple of 14.56x, shares would be trading around $75.70. A notch higher than todays price. Assuming that WPC can beat the midpoint of its guidance and keep trading at its current valuation.

This takes us to the more important topic of today: Why is WPC performing so well relative to the overall REIT market?

CPI-linked rent escalators and high investment volumes are driving the stock

W. P. Carey is a REIT that is investing in new (warehouse & industrial and retail) properties and therefore growing. A growing REIT can boost AFFO per share because of the net-investment spread (expected returns minus weighted average cost of capital used to buy new properties). Portfolio expansion accelerates per-share AFFO growth, leading to a higher multiple. During the Q4 2025 conference call, management said the following regarding investment capitalization rates (cap rates):

Our investments carried a weighted average initial cash cap rate of 7.6% for the year, translating into an average yield just above 9% over long-term leases averaging 17 years. In contrast, the occupied assets we sold, traded at cap rates averaging 6%, generating an average spread of about 150 basis points and creating significant value as we recycled capital from noncore asset sales to higher-yielding net lease investments.

It is important to understand that selling assets at low cap rates in order to buy assets at higher cap rates could be a potential red flag. A higher cap rate basically means that the buyer demands a higher return to justify its purchase price. In most of the cases, this is because the tenant or property is lower-tier. We can find some evidence to back this up by comparing some year-over-year key data from the portfolio.

As you can see, the percentage of average base rent [ABR] coming from investment grade tenants has fallen by 2.3 percentage points [pp] compared to Q4 2024. This resonates well with the fact that new acquisitions have been made possible because of higher net investment spreads. Occupancy dipped 0.6 pp as well. The good thing is that concentration in the top 10 tenants came down a bit (0.5 pp). Better diversification can - to a certain extent - compensate for the added portfolio quality.

I like WPC because of its CPI-linked rent escalators, which are embedded in a large portion of its leases. The consumer price index (CPI) basically equals the rate of inflation. Investing in WPC should provide you with better protection against inflation because of that. We are in a period of higher inflation right now, so cash rent can grow a lot faster when they are CPI-linked. This facilitates a faster-growing dividend rate.

More details regarding rent escalators can be found when comparing the Q4 2024/2025 supplemental presentations:

I don't really like the fact that the amount of leases linked to the CPI has been falling (minus 3 percentage points). It gives me the impression that the high volume of new investments are getting done at the expense of lesser quality tenants and "worse" lease agreements.

WPC seems to be interested in expanding its retail footprint. CEO J. Fox said the following regarding retail investments done during Q4 2025:

In retail, it accounted for about 22% of our deal volume last year, and of course, about 2/3 of that was the lifetime deal. Looking forward to this year, a good chunk of our pipeline is retail. It's probably about half and half right now. Overall, net lease retail is the biggest part of the net lease market, especially in the U.S., and we think it could become a bigger part of our deal volume on an annual basis.

I mean industrial is still really the core part of our business. We want to keep on adding retail, but industrial is significant. It's probably made up 2/3 to maybe even 3/4 of our deal volume over the last number of years in terms of what types of industrial, it's really a mix between both manufacturing and logistics and we do provide some disclosure on those 2 components.

Realty Income (O) is the biggest REIT player in the retail department. Its 2025 same-store rent growth came in at 1.3% (Q4 2025 supplemental, page 6). Especially (uncapped) CPI-linked escalators are just something that we don't really see in retail. So again, WPC is generating AFFO growth by making investments at higher net-investment spreads. This creates a trade-off because you won't get the same level of property/tenant quality or higher rent bumps.

When I see data that could indicate some sort of worrisome "quality deterioration,' I tend to take a look at management compensation. Regarding WPC, you can check this yourself when going to the 2025 proxy statement (page 2).

74% of the CEO's pay mix is coming from long-term equity incentives. This is - among others - based on absolute AFFO per share growth and relative total shareholder return (versus the MSCI US REIT index). None of the CEO compensation is coming from absolute portfolio growth or something comparably bad. When a REIT grows its portfolio using expensive debt/equity, a REIT can theoretically grow without the per share AFFO going up. When looking at management compensation, it is a good sign that there is alignment with shareholders. This makes it easier for me to accept the fact that AFFO growth is coming from new investments at higher cap rates. Yes, there is a trade-off, but management's own compensation is tied to objective performance metrics that include AFFO p/s growth.

Closing remarks

During market turbulence, caused by unnecessary economic (tariffs) or military wars (Iran), investors have a tendency to flight towards investment safe havens. W. P. Carey resonates well with investors in this regard. As I've shown in previous articles, we are talking about a well-run, investment-grade REIT with a management team that is aligned with shareholders.

Starting at a 5% dividend yield combined with a 4.5% dividend increase this year, you can safely say that W. P. Carey can provide you with a more or less double-digit total return. The valuation has been creeping up, but this has also made it possible to make new investments (because of a lower WACC), benefitting net investment spreads.

I think that the reasons to buy, hold or sell this stock haven't materially changed since my Aug. article last year. The same core reasons still apply. Because of the sharp appreciation of the stock, the bull case has been coming down a bit, but this is well compensated by the fact that this is a growing REIT with a great financial profile.

I currently have no shares in W. P. Carey myself, but I'm managing portfolios for my family that do own the company. I don't intend to sell their positions any time soon. For me, the stock is a hold. For retirees or investors with a lower risk appetite, it makes perfect sense to keep expanding your position in this A-tier REIT. When you have a higher risk appetite and you want to capitalize on turnaround or deep-value REITs, there are some that I'm quite bullish on. You can check them out yourselves if you're interested!

As always, thanks for reading.

data.title: McCormick: A Merger With Unilever's Food Business Will Drive Growth
data.description:
McCormick & Company (MKC) is a consumer packaged foods company most dividend growth investors are familiar with. The company has grown organically and through many tuck-in acquisitions, expanding from a player in herbs and spices to adding condiments and flavor solutions. It is now conducting its largest deal ever and will merge with Unilever’s food business. However, a combination of economic malaise and negative sentiment about the merger has caused the share price and valuation to decline since I last wrote about it.

As a dividend growth stock, McCormick is well-known because of its Dividend Aristocrat status and 40-year streak of consecutive annual increases. The share price is now about where it was in mid-2018, but now the dividend yield is 3.53%, almost the highest value in over a decade, and the equity is undervalued based on historical ranges. The merger and global expansion are positives for top and bottom line growth even with greater leverage. Hence, I view McCormick as a long-term buy.

Overview of McCormick

McCormick & Company, Incorporated was founded in 1889 and is headquartered in Hunt Valley, Maryland. Today, it is recognized as the dominant seller of spices, seasonings, and condiments. It operates globally in two segments: Consumer and Flavor Solutions. They both sell spices, seasonings, condiments, flavorings, and other related products. The Consumer segment (61% of total revenue) focuses on retail sales, while the Flavor Solutions segment (39% of total revenue) sells to food manufacturers and food service customers. McCormick owns numerous iconic brands, including McCormick, French’s, Frank’s RedHot, Zatarain’s, Lawry’s, Cholula, Thai Kitchen, Old Bay, and others.

The firm has two classes of shares: a voting class and a non-voting class.

Total revenue was over $6,840 million in fiscal year (“FY”) 2025 and $7,109 million in the last twelve months (“LTM”).

Latest Results

McCormick announced first quarter fiscal year 2026 results on March 31st, 2026. The firm beat revenue and earnings per share (“EPS”) analyst estimates by a solid amount due primarily to adding 25% more of its Mexico joint venture and consolidating reporting results. Revenue increased 16.7% to $1,873.9 million from $1,605.5 million year-over-year, beating estimates by $80 million. Foreign exchange was a tailwind. Adjusted diluted EPS rose by $0.06 to $0.66 from $0.60 in the prior year, ahead of estimates by $0.06. On a GAAP basis, diluted EPS rose to $3.77 due to a non-cash gain from the McCormick de Mexico acquisition, offset by integration charges.

Both segments had higher operating revenues. The Consumer segment’s sales increased 25% to $1,145 million, due mainly to M&A and a 1.8% organic increase. The Flavors Solutions segment had 6% higher sales to $729 million due to M&A and 1% organic growth. Foreign exchange had a 3% favorable impact. The share count was flat.

Consensus analyst estimates are $3.10 per share in FY 2026, up appreciably from $2.93 in fiscal 2025. After the results were released, the share price decreased due to investor selling on the simultaneous announcement of the merger with Unilever’s (UL) food business. The share price has decreased 20.23% year-to-date (“YTD”) and it has declined even more at 27.87% over the LTM.

McCormick's Acquisitions Are Creating a Global Powerhouse

Since my last article, McCormick’s share price has declined to levels last seen before the pandemic, in 2018. Despite decent results in the last two quarters, the firm’s share price has been pressured by fears of inflationary input costs, tariffs, weak consumer spending, disruptions from global conflicts, and now the merger with Unilever’s food business. That said, after an initial steep decline, the share price has recovered, likely because of a more favorable impression of the merger and perceptions that the regional Middle East conflict may slowly abate. Moreover, the increase in ownership to 75% from 50% of the McCormick de Mexico joint venture has seemingly been an operating and financial positive.

McCormick Will Combine with Unilever’s Food Business

McCormick and Unilever announced that they would merge, creating a global flavor company in a $44.8 billion deal. This deal is a complicated one and worth outlining for greater understanding of investor holdings and future growth. It is structured as a Reverse Morris Trust, and Unilever will spin off its food business, which will then be merged with McCormick. McCormick retains its name and executive leadership, despite Unilever’s food business being substantially larger. After the deal is completed, Unilever will have exited the food business in its entirety and become focused on personal care and household goods, akin to Procter & Gamble. McCormick will still be headquartered in Hunt Valley, Maryland, but will have an international headquarters in the Netherlands and possibly a secondary listing in Europe. Although, the new McCormick will be led by its existing management team, Unilever will nominate four directors to the Board, and some of its existing management team will serve in leadership roles.

Apart from the operational terms, Unilever will receive $15.7 billion in cash before closing adjustments. This will likely support its balance sheet and potentially acquisitions to expand its remaining businesses. Unilever will also directly own 9.9% of each share class, giving it a direct interest in the success of the merger. McCormick shareholders will own 35%, while Unilever shareholders will own 55.1% of both share classes in the merged businesses. From a financial perspective, Unilever is definitely benefitting, while McCormick’s total debt and leverage ratio will rise substantially because its enterprise value of about $21 billion is less than half of Unilever’s food business at approximately $44.8 billion. McCormick will increase its debt by $15.7 billion, and the leverage ratio will climb to 4.0X, which is elevated even for a packaged food company. The deal will also result in a $29.1 billion share issuance. Higher debt, leverage, and dilution are the main reasons investors and analysts are mostly negative on the deal. Beyond that, they appear to be concerned about integration risks and poor track records for large M&A deals in the space, such as when Kraft acquired Heinz.

McCormick Will Be a Global Player with Better Growth Potential

Despite these negatives, there are positives for the complex transaction. The deal seems to make sense from a market leadership, brands, geography, and growth perspective. It will create a global player in spices, condiments, and, more broadly, flavors and cooking that has at least $20 billion in annual sales. No other company comes close. Moreover, it will combine McCormick’s strong brands of McCormick spices and herbs, French’s, Frank’s RedHot, and Cholula with Unilever’s major food brands of Hellmann’s, Knorr’s, and Maille. These brands are all number one in their categories. It also brings a host of smaller regional brands with market leadership into the product portfolio. Importantly, there exists little overlap in the product portfolio between the two companies.

This lack of overlap extends in part to geography. McCormick is strong in the United States but arguably has a smaller global footprint. On the other hand, Unilever has both scale and distribution globally, especially in emerging markets, which have growing populations. Similarly, McCormick has a stronger presence in North American consumer retail, while Unilever has a considerable presence in food service.

Importantly, Unilever currently has better operational metrics than McCormick, suggesting they are more efficient. In fiscal year 2025, Unilever’s food business had 2.7% sales growth, while McCormick’s was only 2%. Similarly, Unilever’s food business had higher operating margins of 24% compared to 17% for McCormick. This suggests better growth and margins for the combined entity than McCormick alone. The firm is expecting 3% to 5% sales growth and 23-25% operating margins by year three, better than either McCormick or Unilever before the merger.

Apart from these metrics, the deal is being rationalized with an estimated $600 million in cost synergies, roughly 3% capital expenditures, and a 3.0X leverage ratio by year three. Whether these targets are achieved remains to be seen. Focusing on the leverage ratio, after the addition of a 25% Mexican joint venture, McCormick’s ratio is 3.31X with net debt of $4,746 million. This is higher than desired, but free cash flow is robust, and the firm has demonstrated past capacity to pay down debt. However, the leverage ratio will rise to 4.0X with roughly $20.4 billion of net debt after the merger. The combined company will have robust cash flow and should be able to deleverage. McCormick has been a prudent acquirer in the past and has lowered leverage from elevated values, providing confidence that it will do so again.

Recent Challenges and Risks

McCormick’s primary risks are related to inflation and tariffs. Global inflation after the pandemic has proven to be persistent. Rising labor, freight, energy, and commodity costs must eventually be passed along to customers, which may negatively impact sales volumes. The recent regional conflict in the Middle East has added inflationary pressures as diesel and jet fuel prices are at record highs.

Along the same lines, volatile and unpredictable tariffs are a primary concern. They are constantly changing, and headline values are often not the same ones that take effect. The Supreme Court's recent ruling and the administration's response add more uncertainty. The company must import some of its spices and seasonings, such as vanilla, cloves, turmeric, cinnamon, etc. According to its Q4 FY 2025 update, the company estimates that it has approximately $50 million in tariff exposure, which it can mitigate through price increases, new sourcing, and cost savings. That said, McCormick purchases a broad array of raw materials, limiting its exposure to any single item. Corporations are also trying to recoup money that has already been paid as tariffs.

Additionally, currency fluctuations may impact results, especially if the U.S. dollar weakens or strengthens substantially.

Competitive Advantages

McCormick is the global market leader in terms of market share and revenue. It has about 26% market share of spices and seasonings. In addition, it manufactures and sells both branded and private-label products. The firm also owns well-known brands with leading market share, and often they are number one in their category. It holds approximately 36% of the mustard market share, and is a significant player in the barbecue, hot, and chili sauce markets. The broad portfolio of consumer food products means McCormick occupies a lot of shelf space, creating a barrier to entry.

In addition, McCormick has unmatched scale in its categories, providing operational efficiencies in manufacturing, marketing, distribution, and purchasing that other firms cannot match, especially in the Consumer segment.

Dividend Analysis

Now, let's analyze McCormick’s dividend, including the yield, growth, and safety. The equity is a Dividend Aristocrat and Champion with a 40-year streak of annual increases and solid dividend growth and safety. Moreover, the firm is one that has paid a dividend for 100 years or more. The dividend yield has ranged from approximately 1.2% to 3.8% in the past decade, attaining its maximum in early April 2026 after the merger announcement. Currently, the forward yield is ~3.53%, greater than the 4-year average of about 2.88%, suggesting the equity is undervalued.

McCormick is a Dividend Aristocrat and Champion, boasting an impressive 40-year record of annual dividend increases. The food company has paid dividends since 1925. Over the past 5 years, the growth rate has been around 8.4%. It was slightly more in the past decade, at about 8.67%. That said, I expect growth to decline because of the merger with Unilever’s food business. The firm gave investors a 6.67% quarterly increase to $0.48 per share from $0.45 in November of 2025. I anticipate a small increase in November 2026 because of the payout ratio of 60% and the impending merger resulting in higher leverage. According to Seeking Alpha, the payout ratio ranged from 43% to 60% over the past five years. So, the current value is already at the higher end of the range, implying a slower growth rate in the foreseeable future.

The dividend is supported by solid dividend safety. The forward payout ratio is at around 60%, based on an estimated analyst consensus fiscal 2026 EPS of $3.10 and a dividend rate of $1.92 per share. This value is below my requirement of 65% or better. The ratio is expected to decline in 2026 and 2027, as rising operating revenue and EPS outpace dividend distribution gains. The merger is expected to be accretive in 2027, but I do not expect significant distribution increases. The firm’s free cash flow (“FCF”) has fluctuated in the past three years. It was $973 million in 2023, fell to $647 million in 2024, and rose to $740 million in 2025. The FCF has covered the dividend in each year. Assuming $750 million in FCF and $500 million in dividends this year, due to a higher share count and an increase, produces a dividend-to-FCF ratio of ~66%, which is less than my desired value of 70% or better. I believe McCormick can pay the dividend from its existing FCF.

McCormick's balance sheet is leveraged. The credit rating agencies give the firm a ‘BBB/Baa2’ lower-medium grade investment rating because of its stable cash flows offset by rising input costs and leverage. Examining the numbers from the latest earnings result, the firm has $1,319.3 million of short-term debt and current long-term debt, and $3,604.1 million of long-term debt. It holds about $177.7 million in cash, equivalents, and investments. As a result, the leverage ratio is 3.31X, and interest coverage is 5.85X, which are high for a consumer packaged foods company. These values are also slightly worse than in my last article. Currently, the firm is able to pay the dividend distributions, while managing its debt obligations. The firm is only repurchasing a small quantity of shares each year, and I do not expect that to change after the merger.

My dividend analysis demonstrates that McCormick has solid dividend safety with little possibility of a freeze or cut before the merger. I expect distributions to increase at a low-single-digit rate for the next two years based on the current payout ratio, and FCF, but I anticipate higher leverage.

Valuation

Now, let's analyze McCormick’s valuation and determine if it is a good buy or not at the current share price. Its current share price and projected fiscal year 2026 EPS of $3.10 give the equity a lower valuation relative to its historical average. The stock is valued at ~17.5 times estimated 2026 earnings, which is less than its typical range. According to Seeking Alpha, the 5-year average is closer to 27-28X.

Consensus analyst estimates for diluted adjusted EPS are $3.10 in fiscal 2026, up from $2.93 in 2025. I will use 22X as a realistic, fair-value multiple, accounting for market leadership, brand strength, offset by inflation, tariffs, higher leverage, and merger integration risks. My valuation multiple is lower than in my last article. My assumptions and numbers result in a fair value estimate of $68.20. The current share price is ~$54.33 as of this writing, suggesting McCormick is significantly undervalued.

Applying a sensitivity calculation using P/E ratios between 21X and 23X, I obtain a fair value range from $65.10 to $71.30. Hence, the current stock price is approximately 76% to 83% of the fair value estimate.

Estimated Current Valuation Based On P/E Ratio

How does this calculation compare to other valuation targets? Wall Street analysts are less bullish, with a price target of $62.69, which is less than my valuation estimate but 15.39% higher than the current share price. Moreover, analysts are optimistic in their ratings, with five strong buys, three buys, seven holds, zero sells, and zero strong sells. However, the Seeking Alpha Quant system rating is a ‘sell’ due to unfavorable valuation, growth, momentum, and revision factors. However, the stock has a favorable profitability factor.

Final Thoughts

McCormick is a well-known consumer packaged food company with market leadership in herbs, spices, and condiments. Its brands are well-known and growing. The firm is merging with Unilever’s food business, which also owns familiar brands. The combination will result in a global leader. Both firms have long histories as dividend growth stocks, which should continue after the merger. Currently, the market is undervaluing the potential positives because of the integration risks and more leverage, compounded by inflationary, tariff, and global conflict risks. However, the firm has solid dividend safety metrics, a nearly decade-high yield, a 40-year streak of annual increases, and a long-term growth profile. Despite higher leverage and merger integration risk, this stock should be attractive to many investors for their dividend growth portfolios, especially given its undervaluation, dividend growth rate, and anticipation of higher growth rates and operating margins. I view McCormick as a buy.

data.title: Janus Henderson AAA CLO ETF Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
At a glance

Performance

The Fund returned 0.91% and the J.P. Morgan CLO AAA Index returned 1.01%.

Contributors/detractors

Asset allocation performed broadly in line with the benchmark, while yield-curve positioning contributed modestly and security selection detracted.

Outlook

Against a backdrop of heightened geopolitical uncertainty, we believe AAA rated collateralized loan obligations (CLOs) offer investors a compelling combination of floating-rate diversification and strong credit quality.

Portfolio management

John Kerschner, CFA

Nick Childs

CFA Jessica Shill

Investment environment

  • The U.S. AAA CLO market posted a positive quarterly return, contrasting with the weakness seen across bond markets as war in Iran sent oil prices soaring, altering the outlook for inflation and interest rates.
  • The Federal Reserve (Fed) kept the fed funds rate unchanged at 3.50% to 3.75%, while noting the “uncertain” implications of the Middle East conflict. Having previously discounted two or three more cuts in 2026, futures markets now are anticipating no change in Fed policy. The yield curve flattened out meaningfully as 2-year Treasury yields rose more than 10-year yields due to markets repricing rate-cut expectations.
  • U.S. employment data was soft in February, with job creation in 2025 revised sharply down and nonfarm payrolls falling. A strong bounce-back in job creation and a drop in the unemployment rate followed in March, indicating that the labor market continues to tread water. Inflation readings were steady, with the consumer price index (CPI) coming in at 2.4% year-on-year in February.
  • The U.S. 2-year Treasury yield ended the quarter 32 basis points (BPs) higher, at 3.79%. Spreads on AAA CLOs ended the quarter three bps wider, at 134 bps.

Portfolio review

The CLO market proved remarkably resilient during a volatile quarter for fixed income markets, with a record-breaking $84 billion in secondary trading and AAA tranche valuations holding close to historically tight levels.

Concerns about the possible disintermediation effects of artificial intelligence (AI) cast a shadow over credit sectors seen as most exposed to the risk – notably software – leading to greater dispersion of performance. Negative sentiment later fanned out to other sectors as the idea took hold that interest rates might have to stay higher for longer due to the surge in oil prices. With investors repositioning for risk but not shifting en masse to cash, CLOs held up better than most other markets, demonstrating the asset class’s defensive characteristics.

Our CLO exposure to the software sector is roughly in line with the benchmark and market median. The broader CLO market also offers greater diversification and a lower risk of default than loans, which, at up to 25% of the total, has roughly double the exposure of CLOs to sectors considered vulnerable to AI disintermediation effects – and even more in the case of private credit. In this environment, CLO spreads are modestly wider than the tightest levels seen earlier this year.

Although investor demand is concentrated in shorter-duration AAA paper, we see opportunities to selectively add higher-risk tranches where we believe spreads have widened excessively. In that respect, we paused our move into junior AAA tranches earlier in the year and selectively began increasing allocations to AA rated CLOs. In our view, the spread between these and the top end of the market has grown too wide, and so we see an opportunity pick up additional yield for only a modest increase in credit risk.

While this move down the capital stack was initially U.S.-focused, we since have shifted our attention to European CLOs as the market has begun to penalize the region for its greater dependency on oil imports. We see compelling relative value in European AA-rated and junior AAA tranches and expect to grow the portfolio's allocation to the region, as circumstances allow. Senior CLOs, particularly in Europe, offer compelling relative value with short duration and spreads competitive with investment-grade corporates despite lower credit risk.

Manager outlook

Short-term inflation expectations have increased meaningfully because of the jump in energy prices amid the Iran conflict. While the market aligns with our views in appearing to assume the situation will not get much worse from here, inflation expectations suggest the market expects energy prices to remain materially above pre-war levels for some time, with only about one-third of the increase in prices likely to reverse.

Looking ahead, we expect fixed income markets to remain volatile but constructive. Attractive starting yields provide an income cushion, while economic growth appears resilient on the back of the tailwinds of AI-infrastructure spending, strong corporate fundamentals, and lower taxes under the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA), and should be supportive of spread levels within AAA CLOs.

The path of inflation amid energy-related pressures and Fed policy likely will remain key drivers of market direction. The conflict in Iran remains the primary near-term risk to the markets, and while we are encouraged by a desire and incentive to de-escalate, there is an uncertain path to get there. We believe the administration will be motivated to reduce these pressures given the impact of higher energy prices and interest rates on consumers heading into mid-term elections.

Against a backdrop of heightened geopolitical uncertainty and AI disintermediation, we believe AAA CLOs offer investors a compelling combination of floating-rate diversification and strong credit quality.

Definitions

Basis point (BP) equals 1/100 of a percentage point. 1 bp = 0.01%, 100 bps = 1%.

Carry is the excess income earned from holding a higher-yielding security relative to another.

Credit quality ratings are measured on a scale that generally ranges from AAA (highest) to D (lowest).

Credit spread is the difference in yield between securities with similar maturity but different credit quality. Widening spreads generally indicate deteriorating creditworthiness of corporate borrowers, and narrowing indicate improving.

Duration measures a bond price's sensitivity to changes in interest rates. The longer a bond's duration, the higher its sensitivity to changes in interest rates and vice versa.

Tranches are segments of a pool of securities that are divided up by credit rating, maturity, or other characteristics.

data.title: Turbocharging Energy Stocks With The ERX ETF
data.description:
In an August 7, 2025, Seeking Alpha article on the Direxion Daily Energy 2X Shares (ERX), I concluded with the following:

Steady oil prices in August 2025, and the U.S. administration’s support for fossil fuels through its “drill-baby-drill” and “frack-baby-frack” policies, support U.S. oil and oil-related companies’ earnings. Trading these volatile companies using ERX and ERY with time and price stops could generate significant results.

NYMEX crude oil futures were trading below $65 per barrel, the XLE ETF was at $42.20, and the ERX ETF was at $50.67 on August 7. The events in late February and March caused all three to rally and remain higher than the August 7 price on April 20.

Energy was the best-performing sector in Q1 2026

The Middle East conflict lifted traditional energy prices over the first three months of 2026. The energy composite posted a 55.71% gain from the end of 2025 through the end of March 2026. The individual Q1 2026 gains were as follows:

  • NYMEX WTI and ICE Brent crude oil futures rallied 76.56% and 70.73%, respectively.
  • NYMEX RBOB gasoline futures rose by 86.82%. Gasoline refining spreads moved 127.91% higher.
  • NYMEX heating oil futures, which are a proxy for other distillate oil products, posted a 93.91% gain. Distillate refining spreads gained 123.5%.
  • Chicago ethanol swaps rose 27.99%.
  • Coal for delivery in Rotterdam, the Netherlands, rose 26.1%.
  • Coal for delivery in Newcastle, Australia, rose 32.5%.

The only energy commodity to decline in Q1 2026 was U.S. natural gas, which fell 21.76% as the market moved from peak demand to the shoulder season of low demand. However, European natural gas futures moved higher, with U.K. natural gas prices posting a 75.55% gain, and Dutch natural gas prices moving 80.24% higher.

The XLE rose over 37% in Q1 but has pulled back in early Q2

The fund profile for the State Street Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE) states:

XLE’s top holdings include:

Rising energy prices boosted the shares of energy-related companies and the XLE ETF in Q1.

The monthly chart shows that the XLE rose 37% in Q1 2026, moving from $44.71 to $61.26 per share. The XLE fell below $56 per share on April 20, 2026, as energy prices declined amid prospects for an end to hostilities in the Middle East.

U.S. oil companies could remain big winners after the war with Iran

The reasons why U.S. energy companies and the XLE should continue to profit after the war with Iran ends are as follows:

  • The United States is the leading oil and gas-producing country.
  • The U.S. administration supports increasing oil and gas production to achieve energy independence and lead the world in exports.
  • Sanctions on Russia and supply concerns over Middle Eastern oil and gas make U.S. energy more attractive.

U.S. oil and gas producers and related companies in oil services and refining should continue to profit over the coming months and years.

ERX turbocharges the XLE

The fund summary for the Direxion Daily Energy Bull 2X ETF (ERX) states:

ERX’s top holdings include:

ERX’s holdings include many of the energy companies held by the XLE ETF. In Q1 2026, the XLE rose by 37%.

The chart shows that the leveraged ERX ETF rose 84.4%, moving from $55.85 to $103.01 per share. The gain for ERX more than doubled the gain of XLE for the first quarter of 2026.

Both the XLE and ERX corrected since the end of Q1. At around $55.50 per share on April 20, the XLE was 9.4% lower, while at around $84.20, the leveraged ERX ETF fell by 18.3%.

At $84.20 per share, ERX had $244.58 million in assets under management. ERX trades an average of 479,972 shares per day and charges a 0.91% management fee. The $1.49 dividend yields 1.77%.

Seeking Alpha’s ETF grades for ERX are the highest possible A+ in the momentum category due to its price action over the past few months. While ERX earns a B+ in the liquidity category, dividends receive a D+, and the 0.91% management fee receives a D- in the expense category. ERX’s leverage and extreme volatility in energy commodities and related stocks earn a failing grade in the risk category.

The Direxion Daily Energy Bear 2X ETF (ERY) is ERX’s converse product that rises when traditional energy-related stocks decline.

At $12.46 per share, ERY has moved higher since the end of Q1 when ERX posted a 18.3% decline.

The daily chart shows the 21.2% gain from $10.28 on March 31, 2026, to $12.46 per share on April 21, 2026.

The potential for high rewards comes with high risks

Leverage comes at a steep price: time decay. If energy-related stocks decline or remain stable, ERX will lose value. If energy-related stocks rally or remain stable, ERY will lose value.

ERX, ERY, and other leveraged ETFs are tools suitable only for short-term risk positions on the long or short side of energy-related stocks. When approaching energy share exposure with ERX or ERY, time and price stops are appropriate, as the gearing can lead to oversized losses. Moreover, stable energy share prices will likely cause ERX and ERY to decline, as time decay erodes their value. Swaps and options that create leverage dramatically increase risk. The SEC website highlights the significant risks associated with leveraged ETFs, such as ERX and ERX.

Leverage requires planning. Any risk position in ERX or ERY requires stops and profit horizons that reflect the ETF’s potential for extreme volatility. While it is appropriate to increase profit horizons when a leveraged ETF moves in the anticipated direction, adjusting stop levels will protect profits and capital. When ERX or ERY move contrary to expectations, sticking to stop levels will protect capital and limit losses.

I am rating ERX a hold in the current environment, as both ERX and ERY are trading tools and not investment tools. I favor ERX, as there is a compelling case for continued profits in energy-related stocks.
